coredata.dc:title: Ratiometric electrochemiluminescence lab-on-paper device for DNA methylation determination based on highly conductive copper paper electrode
coredata.dc:creator: Lv, Xue
Zhu, Lin
Rong, Yumeng
Shi, Huihui
Zhang, Lina
Yu, Jinghua
Zhang, Yan
coredata.dc:description: Herein, a ratiometric electrochemiluminescence (ECL) lab-on-paper platform, based on enhancing effect of the N-(4-aminobutyl)-N-ethylisoluminol-functionalized glutathione-coated cobalt/gold bimetallic nanoclusters (GCAL) and quenching effect of carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as well as benefiting from high conductivity copper paper electrode, was developed to achieve accurate and sensitive analysis of DNA methylation. Concretely, due to one-to-multiple amplification effect, one target DNA could initiate the formation of supersandwich structure with multiple signal probes labeled with GCAL. Thus, the GCAL signal enhancement effect was realized. Simultaneously, multiple signal probes are connected to hinder the electron conduction rate on the electrode surface, which results in the quenching of CQDs signal. With the method proposed here, wide linear relationship in the range of 1 fM to 10 pM with a low detection limit of 0.27 fM for sensitive detecting methylated DNA was achieved. It is believed that this work provided a rapid, low-cost and stabilized method for specific determination of methylated DNA, and the immediate detection of cancer in the clinic will become possible in the future.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric
Lab-on-paper
Electrochemiluminescence
Copper
DNA methylation

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid orcid primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of dual-potential ratiometric ECL integrated LOP device
Fabrication of Au@rGO on the paper-based Cu working electrode
Preparation of probe DNA labeled GCAL nanohybrid
Testing of the ratiometric ECL lab-on-paper device

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Schematic layout, size, and shape of the integrated LOP device. (B) Photographs of the integrated LOP device after modification with CuNPs. (C) Folding process and the integrated LOP device assembled with the aid of crocodile clips.
SEM images of (A, B) bare paper surface, (C, D) PCWE, (E, F) rGO/PCWE, and (G, H) Au@rGO/PCWE.
(A) Wide scan XPS spectra of PCWE and (B) high-resolution XPS spectra of Cu 2p of PCWE. (C) XRD patterns of PCWE and Au@rGO/PCWE. (D) Raman spectra of Au@rGO/PCWE.
(A) TEM images of CQDs and (D) HRTEM images of GCAL. FL emission spectra of (B) CQDs and (E) GCAL. UV–vis spectra of (C) CQDs and (F) ABEI, GCA, GCAL.

(A) ECL responses of the proposed device to different concentrations of methylated target DNA (a–f: 0 fM, 1 fM, 10 fM, 100 fM, 1 pM, and 10 pM). (B) ECL intensities of CQDs and GCAL corresponding to the logarithm of target DNA concentrations. (C) Calibration curve of ECLGCAL/ECLCQDs for methylated target DNA detection. (D) Stability, (E) selectivity (a–h: Na+, Ca2+, K+, Zn2+, hemoglobin, myoglobin, thrombin and target DNA), and (F) reproducibility of the ratiometric ECL integrated LOP device.
Fabrication procedures of the ratiometric ECL integrated LOP device.

head.ce:title: Ratiometric electrochemiluminescence lab-on-paper device for DNA methylation determination based on highly conductive copper paper electrode

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan, 250022, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan 250022, P.R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shandong Provincial Key Laboratory of Preparation and Measurement of Building Materials, University of Jinan, Jinan, 250022, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shandong Provincial Key Laboratory of Preparation and Measurement of Building Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shandong Provincial Key Laboratory of Preparation and Measurement of Building Materials, University of Jinan, Jinan 250022, P.R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, a ratiometric electrochemiluminescence (ECL) lab-on-paper platform, based on enhancing effect of the N-(4-aminobutyl)-N-ethylisoluminol-functionalized glutathione-coated cobalt/gold bimetallic nanoclusters (GCAL) and quenching effect of carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as well as benefiting from high conductivity copper paper electrode, was developed to achieve accurate and sensitive analysis of DNA methylation. Concretely, due to one-to-multiple amplification effect, one target DNA could initiate the formation of supersandwich structure with multiple signal probes labeled with GCAL. Thus, the GCAL signal enhancement effect was realized. Simultaneously, multiple signal probes are connected to hinder the electron conduction rate on the electrode surface, which results in the quenching of CQDs signal. With the method proposed here, wide linear relationship in the range of 1 fM to 10 pM with a low detection limit of 0.27 fM for sensitive detecting methylated DNA was achieved. It is believed that this work provided a rapid, low-cost and stabilized method for specific determination of methylated DNA, and the immediate detection of cancer in the clinic will become possible in the future.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: DNA methylation is an important epigenetic modification that results from 5-methylcytosine catalyzed by the DNA methyltransferase (Gao et al., 2020; Lyko, 2018), especially the aberrant methylation occurs in gene promoter regions. Numerous studies have verified that DNA methylation is implicated in the pathogenesis of atherosclerosis, Parkinson, diabetes, cancer, and others (Greenberg, 2019; Schubeler, 2015). Hence, the accurate detection of DNA methylation is increasingly recognized as an important determinant in diagnosis and therapy of most diseases (Chen et al., 2022; Zhu et al., 2022). In recent years, plentiful analytical techniques have been widely used to precisely identify DNA methylation, including chromatography-based techniques, sequencing analysis, methylation-specific polymerase chain reaction (Wu et al., 2017), etc. However, most of the methods mentioned above were generally limited by several crucial factors, such as complicated operations, expensive instrumentation and skilled personnel. Consequently, it is significant to establish a sensitive, reliable, and portable device for rapid and on-site determination of methylation in CpG dinucleotides.

Recently, the lab-on-paper (LOP) device has attracted substantial attention in the point-of-care testing field, with the inherent merits of cellulose paper, such as high porosity, cost-effective, and portable (Hou et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2018b; Zheng et al., 2020). Usually, in order to ameliorate the performance of the integrated LOP device, a variety of materials are used for modifying cellulose fibers to improve their conductivity, such as carbon or noble metal. Au nanoparticles (AuNPs) have drew increasing attention in the domain of biosensing and analysis for the past few years, as a typical noble metal, which possess the merits of efficient conductivity, excellent biocompatibility, and long-term stability (Li et al., 2020; Yang et al., 2018; Zhang et al. 2018a, 2021). However, on account of expensive price of noble metal, which limit their applications on the large-scale. Carbon nanomaterials are also commonly used to modify paper fibers, in spite of comparatively low-cost, but their conductivity is not satisfactory (Kong et al., 2018; Yang et al., 2021). Remarkably, copper (Cu) not only possess the second highest electrical conductivity among metals, but also the price is far below than that of the noble metals. Particularly, it can be electrolessly deposited onto cellulose fibers, forming copper paper with plentiful fascinating properties, such as high conductivity with a sheet resistance much less than frequently-used indium tin oxide, as well as superior stability due to the strong anchoring strength between the copper and paper fibers. Furthermore, the characteristic nano- and micro-structures of the copper make it with highly hydrophobic and high photothermal conversion efficiency, thus have infinite potency in improving the performance of the integrated LOP device.

Recently, multiple technologies have combined with LOP device for detecting multifarious biomolecules, including colorimetric (Ali et al., 2019), electrochemical (Liu et al., 2022b; Zhou et al., 2021), photoelectrochemical (Zhao et al., 2021a; Zhao et al., 2021b), and electrochemiluminescence (Zhu et al., 2021a). Especially, electrochemiluminescence (ECL) technology, as a powerful analysis technique, integrates the advantages of electrochemistry and chemiluminescence (Cao et al., 2021), has been widely used in environmental monitoring (Xu et al., 2018), food hygiene safety (Yang et al., 2015), clinical diagnosis (Yang et al., 2021) and other fields, due to its high sensitivity, low background, high controllability, and good reproducibility (Liu et al., 2022; Tian et al., 2021; Yan et al., 2021). Accordingly, ECL technology has been incorporated into microfluidic paper-based analysis equipment for bioanalysis owing to its collapsible, cost-efficient, and good biocompatibility (Wang et al., 2020). Moreover, with the help of inkjet printing and screen-printing technology, microfluidic paper-based ECL detection device can realize the functionalization of different areas, containing working electrode, reference electrode, channel zone, etc (Huang et al., 2019). Although various ECL biosensors with single-signal measurement have achieved satisfactory results in certain degree (Huang et al. 2021a, 2021b), which still suffer from false positive or false negative errors due to the influence of instrument efficiency or some external environment factors especially in the analysis of complex biological circumstances. As a consequence, it would be a judicious choice to construct the double-signal based strategies (Hong et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2020). The “dual-signal” ratiometric measurement through the self-calibration of two different luminophors (He et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2018b), can greatly eliminate the numerous external environmental effects and provide more persuasive analysis results, enabling ratiometric biosensors to work in more realistic and complex surroundings (Fang et al., 2019). Moreover, QDs have attracted considerable attention in biosensing, which attributed to their unique properties, such as controllability and biocompatibility, etc. Compared with the metal-based QDs such as CdSe and CdTe, CQDs possessed fascinating optical properties and low toxicity, including photoluminescence, chemiluminescence, especially the ECL. At present, they have become a hot topic in ECL luminophores research (Chen et al., 2021; Fang et al., 2022; Jampasa et al., 2021).

In this work, an innovative dual-potential ratiometric ECL lab-on-paper device was established to precisely monitor DNA methylation based on enhancing effect of the N-(4-aminobutyl)-N-ethylisoluminol-functionalized glutathione-coated cobalt/gold bimetallic nanoclusters (GCAL) and quenching effect of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) (the detailed fabrication procedures of the proposed LOP device are displayed in Scheme 1 ). Primarily, paper fibers were modified with copper nanoparticles (CuNPs), reduced graphene oxide (rGO) and Au nanoparticles (AuNPs) in the detection zone through electroless deposition, hydrothermal and in-situ growth methods, respectively. Then, CQDs were cast onto the detection zone followed by the addition of chitosan. Next, AuNPs grown on the electrode surface provide abundant binding sites for the immobilization of capture DNA. Then, the target DNA was stably introduced into the electrode surface by partial hybridization with the capture DNA. Subsequently, probe GCAL was further hybridized with the target DNA, and in the presence of assistant DNA, the supersandwich structure was successfully fabricated. As revealed in Scheme S1, one target DNA could initiate the cascading hybridization reaction between the probe GCAL and assistant DNA. Therefore, with the increasing of target DNA concentration, the amount of captured probe GCAL also increased, resulting in the anode ECL signal of GCAL enhanced accordingly. Meanwhile, the cathode ECL signal of the CQDs quenched for the reason that electron conduction rate on the electrode surface was affected by cascading hybridization reaction in the detection zone. On the basis of the ECLGCAL/ECLCQDs values, the integrated LOP device could avoid false positive signal primely as well as exhibits excellent performance in the detection of DNA methylation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of dual-potential ratiometric ECL integrated LOP device
ce:section.ce:para: First of all, the unique pattern was drawn by Adobe Illustrator CS6 software, and printed on A4 size Whatman No.2 chromatography paper using the wax printer. Afterwards, the papers were placed into an oven at 130 °C for 90 s, the wax was melted and formed a three-dimensional hydrophobic pattern. As exhibited in Fig. 1 A, the integrated LOP device was composed of three pads. Pad I with a circular reservoir (10.0 mm in diameter) was used for increasing the amount of liquid stored. Pad II was consisted of a hydrophilic circular zone and a semi-hydrophilic zone to generate a controllable circular zone, and the rest of the hydrophilic area was functionalized with CuNPs as working electrode (Fig. 1B). In addition, the Ag/AgCl reference electrode and carbon counter electrode were screen-printed on the pad III. Next, each individual functional unit was cut from the paper sheet to construct independent facility. Then, the ECL detection zone of the pad II was modified with rGO and AuNPs. Eventually, these pads were stacked together to establish a three-dimensional multifunctional integrated LOP device in the predefined order according to the principle of origami, as illustrated in Fig. 1C. Consequently, the detection zone, the reservoir zone and the reference zone could be connected after the regent solution was added into the detection zone to realize accurate ECL detection of the target.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of Au@rGO on the paper-based Cu working electrode
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, CuNPs were grown in situ on the surface of cellulose fibers via electroless deposition approach. Concretely, 100 μL 1 mg mL−1 dopamine solution (pH 8.5, 10 mM tris (hydroxymethyl) aminomethane (Tris) buffer) was firstly dripped on the hydrophilic area of the ECL detection tab to form a thin adhesive dopamine film through the interaction between the catechol group of dopamine and the hydroxyl group exposed in cellulose paper. Then, ammonium tetrachloropalladate ((NH4)2PdCl, 0.5 mg mL−1, 50 μL) was added to the dopamine-functionalized cellulose paper region. After 60 min of reaction, it was rinsed repeatedly with deionized (DI) water. Next, copper nanoparticles were electrolessly deposited on the surface of the cellulose. Specifically, 100 μL prepared mixture was dropped into the hydrophilic area of the ECL detection tab which containing sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 13 mg mL−1), copper sulfate pentahydrate (CuSO4⋅5H2O, 15 mg mL−1), potassium sodium tartrate tetrahydrate (C4H4O6KNa⋅4H2O, 30 mg mL−1) and 200 μL formaldehyde (HCHO) solution. After reaction for 20 min, all the samples were washed with DI water and dried at 50 °C vacuum oven.

Subsequently, the rGO was synthesized by hydrothermal method according to the reported literature with some modification (Liu et al., 2014). Firstly, the aqueous graphene oxide (GO) dispersions (0.8 mg mL−1) was dropped on the ECL detection zone, and dried at room temperature. The above procedures were repeated for three times. Then, the GO modified paper was put into the autoclave containing hydrazine monohydrate (50 μL, 80 wt%), ammonia solution (44 μL, 28 wt%) and GO aqueous dispersion (12 mL, 0.8 mg mL−1). Next, the Teflon-lined autoclave was heated in an oven at 100 °C for 2 h to convert GO to rGO on paper-based Cu working electrode (PCWE). Finally, the rGO/PCWE was obtained by washing with DI water and natural drying. Then, AuNPs were grown on the surface of rGO/PCWE by direct reduction of tetrachloroauric acid (HAuCl4) with L-ascorbic acid, according to the method previously reported (Wang et al., 2018a). First of all, freshly prepared AuNPs seed solution (80 μL) was applied to the rGO/PCWE surface and placed at room temperature for 1 h. The above steps were repeated three times to optimize the immobilization of AuNPs on the modified electrode surface. Secondly, a mixture of L-ascorbic acid (100 mmol L−1) and HAuCl4 solution (15 mmol L−1) was mixed in equal volume and quickly added to the modified electrode surface. After reaction for 10 min at room temperature, the obtained Au@rGO/PCWE was washed with DI water and dried naturally.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of probe DNA labeled GCAL nanohybrid
ce:section.ce:para: In order to obtain GCAL nanohybrid (the detailed preparing processes could be found in Supplementary Material) labeled DNA signal probe, 50 μL probe DNA (10 μM) was treated with Tris (2-carboxyethyl) phosphine (TCEP, 10 mM) and incubated for 1 h at room temperature to reduce disulfide bonds. Followed by mixing with 1 mL GCAL nanoparticles solution prepared above, and incubated for 4 h at room temperature. After centrifugation, the obtained precipitates were suspended with 200 μL Tris-HCl buffer (10 mM, pH 8.0). Sequentially, 25 μL 6-mercapto-1-hexanol (MCH, 1 mM) solution was added and incubated for 1 h at room temperature to block the probe GCAL conjugates and optimize the orientation of probe DNA, preventing the nonspecific adsorption of the glutathione-coated cobalt/gold bimetallic nanoclusters (GCA) surface and making the hybridization easier.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Testing of the ratiometric ECL lab-on-paper device
ce:section.ce:para: Initially, CuNPs were grown on the hydrophilic area of the ECL detection tab, endowing the paper working electrode with a large effective surface area and excellent conductivity. Next, a layer of rGO and AuNPs were coated on the detection zone. Then, 30 μL CQDs (detail could be found in Supplementary Material) was applied to the rGO surface, followed by the addition of 10 μL chitosan (0.07 wt%) to stabilize the electrode. After that, 100 μL TCEP (10 mM) were added into 100 μL capture DNA (2 μM) and incubated for 30 min to activate it. Next, 20 μL above solution was dropped onto the detection zone and incubated for 12 h at room temperature. Subsequently, the electrode was passivated by dropping 20 μL MCH (1 mM) to block the nonspecific adsorption sites, and washed with 20 mM PBS solution (pH 7.4) to remove the unbounded substance.

Firstly, the target DNA was pretreated with bisulfite, after that, 20 μL target DNA solution was introduced into the detection zone. Two hours later, 20 μL solution containing probe GCAL (1 μM) and assistant DNA (1 μM) was added to the detection zone for an additional 75 min, and the ECL LOP device was assembled. The methylated DNA-initiated one-to-multiple amplification was demonstrated by polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis analysis (Fig. S1). And the feasibility of the proposed integrated lab-on-paper device was further confirmed by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy measurement (Fig. S2). Ultimately, ECL responses were recorded on a MPI-B ECL analyzer in PBS buffer (pH 7.6, 100 mM) containing H2O2 and S2O8 2-, and the potential scanning was range from - 2.0 to + 0.5 V.

A multilayered paper chip based on highly conductive copper paper electrode was designed to realize ratiometric ECL assay, which can effectively complete liquid flowing through extensive channels in the process of practical application. Initially, CuNPs were successfully grown on the tangled cellulose fibers network by electroless deposition method to enhance the electrical conductivity of the paper-based working electrode, which plays a crucial role in sensing analysis. Next, a layer of rGO and AuNPs were coated on the detection zone, which affords an excellent attachment microenvironment. As exhibited in Fig. S3, the sheet resistance of PCWE as low as 0.1 Ω sq−1, which is far below than the sheet resistance of paper-based Au working electrode (Zhu et al., 2021b) and fluorine doped tin oxide coated glass (FTO). Besides, scanning electron microscopy (SEM) can prove that the rough surface of bare paper with a multi-void structure in Fig. 2 A and B, which provides a good microenvironment for the adhesion of CuNPs. Preliminary observation shows that the dense CuNPs are uniformly attached to the paper substrate surface in Fig. 2C and D, demonstrating the successful deposition of CuNPs on the paper fibers. Then, to further enhance its electrical conductivity, rGO was synthesized on the surface of PCWE. As shown in Fig. 2E and F, the magnified image indicated that rGO coated on the PCWE presents a corrugated flakelike structure which could provide plentiful active sites for the growth of AuNPs. As presented in Fig. 2G and H, AuNPs are densely and evenly distributed on the surface of rGO, providing a suitable microenvironment for biological experiments.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was employed to characterize the morphology and size of CQDs. As shown in Fig. 4 A, CQDs exhibited an approximately spherical shape, and the particle size distribution range from 3 to 7 nm. The high resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) images indicated that a homogeneous distribution of the GCAL with the size of 1–2 nm (Fig. 4D). In addition, fluorescence (FL) and UV–vis absorption spectra were performed to manifest the optical properties of CQDs and GCAL. The FL emission spectra of CQDs was shown in Fig. 4B, as the excitation wavelength increases from 310 nm to 380 nm, the emission wavelength of CQDs first increases and then decreases. Meanwhile, the location of the emission wavelengths were distinctly redshifted from the original position of 432 – 465 nm. This phenomenon is probably involved in the uneven size distribution and surface passivation of CQDs (Fang et al., 2017). Similarly, the emission spectra of GCAL at different excitation wavelengths was obtained. As exhibited in Fig. 4E, the fluorescence of GCAL first increases and then decreases and reaches the maximum at 420 nm with the excitation wavelength from increasing 280 – 350 nm. The successful synthesis of CQDs and GCAL were also demonstrated by UV absorption spectra. As described in Fig. 4C, the synthesized CQDs shows a characteristic peak at 338 nm, which is attributed to a transition to the direction of the sp2 domain. In contrast, it could be seen that the characteristic absorption peaks of N-(4-aminobutyl)-N-ethylisoluminol (ABEI) appeared at about 290 and 320 nm (Fig. 4F). And the UV–vis absorption spectrum of GCA only exhibited a weak broad peak at about 390 nm. However, the UV–Vis absorption peak of GCAL at about 320 nm was corresponding to the characteristic absorption peak of ABEI, demonstrating that the presence of ABEI in the structure of GCAL. Moreover, the surface plasmon resonance characteristic peak of GCA at about 520 nm disappeared from the absorption spectrum, indicating that large-size gold nanoparticles were not formed during the preparation process. The elemental composition of the GCAL was also characterized by inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometer, which confirmed the presence of Au and Co elements, with concentrations of 0.299 and 0.022 mg mL−1, respectively. All of the above results demonstrated that the GCAL has been successfully prepared.

In order to accomplish the best analytical performance of the proposed ratiometric ECL device, crucial experimental parameter was investigated that the effect of the co-reactant concentration. Firstly, the influence of one co-reactant (such as S2O8 2-) on another luminophore (GCAL) is explored. The constructed LOP device were separately scanned in PBS buffer solution (pH 7.4) only containing H2O2 (100 mM) or S2O8 2- (100 mM). As exhibited in Fig. S4A, the result of potential scanning in PBS buffer solution containing H2O2 shows that the signal response of GCAL appeared when the voltage is +0.5 V, but there is no signal of CQDs. In the same way, the signal response of CQDs appeared when the voltage was −2 V in the PBS buffer solution containing S2O8 2-, but no signal of GCAL. Hence, the influence of one co-reactant on the other luminophore can be neglected. Then the concentrations of S2O8 2- and H2O2 were optimized, respectively. As described in Fig. S4B, the ECL response generated by CQDs increased with the increasing concentration of S2O8 2- ranging from 60 to 140 mM, and reached the peak at 100 mM. When the concentration over 100 mM, the ECL intensity decreased. Therefore, 100 mM was selected as the optimal S2O8 2- concentration to enhance the sensitivity of the integrated LOP device. Similarly, 80 mM was selected as the optimal H2O2 concentration (Fig. S4C). The pH value of the PBS buffer solution is another important parameter, which affects the analytical performance of laboratory equipment on paper. It is noted that the ECL intensity ratio first sharply increases when the pH value changing from 6.0 to 7.6, then slowly decreases and stabilizes at higher values (Fig. S4D). Therefore, 7.6 was regarded as the optimal pH value to improve the sensitivity of the constructed proportional ECL LOP device. Moreover, it is significant for the ECL system to choose the appropriate scanning rate. Under the above optimum conditions, while keeping other parameters unchanged, the influence of scanning rate on ECL intensity ratio was investigated. As revealed in Fig. S4E, the ECL intensity reached the maximum value when the scanning rate is 0.3 V s−1. Besides, the incubation time of the signal probe is also a vital element that affects the performance of the device. Fig. S4F shows that the ECL signal intensity ratio first gradually increases and then decreases with the increasing incubation time. At the time of 75 min, the maximum signal intensity ratio was obtained. Therefore, 75 min was selected as the optimum incubation time for the signal probe.

As mentioned above, the optimal conditions for DNA methylation detection are: (i) the concentration of S2O8 2- is 100 mM, and H2O2 is 80 mM; (ii) the pH value of the PBS buffer solution is 7.6; (iii) the scanning rate is 0.3 V s−1; (iv) the optimal incubation time of probe GCAL is 75 min. Under the optimal conditions, as described above, the relationship between ECL signal value and methylated target DNA concentration was shown by the ECL intensity-time curve. As demonstrated in Fig. 5 A, it is visibly seen that the cathodic ECL intensity from CQDs decreased while the anodic ECL intensity from GCAL increased simultaneously with increasing target DNA concentrations. In Fig. 5B, the ECL intensity changes of the cathode (black line) and anode (red line) with the logarithm of the target DNA concentration. Fig. 5C exhibited a good linear relationship between the logarithm value of ECLGCAL/ECLCQDs and the logarithm value of the target DNA concentration in the range of 1 fM to 10 pM. The linear equation is lg(ECLGCAL/ECLCQDs) = 0.2385lgc + 1.0737 (R2 = 0.9943), where c is the concentration of the methylated target DNA, and the limit of detection is 0.27 fM. In addition, the detection performance of the ratiometric ECL integrated LOP device was compared with various previously reported strategies, including fluorescent, SERS, ECL, electrochemical, SPR detection, as illustrated in Table S1. Distinctly, it could be seen that the proposed method possessed better application potential for DNA methylation detection than that of others.

Given the stability is an important factor in evaluating sensor performance, it can be evaluated by consecutive cyclic potential scanning of the LOP device incubated with 1 pM methylated target DNA. There was negligible changes in the obtained ECL response at each scanning cycle, exhibiting outstanding stability of the proposed ECL device for target DNA surveillance (Fig. 5D). In consideration of complicated physiological environment where the integrated LOP device are used in practical applications, the selectivity of the proposed equipment was tested by Na+, Ca2+, K+, Zn2+, myoglobin, hemoglobin, thrombin. As illustrated in Fig. 5E, it was revealed that the ECL response generated by interfering ions and proteins with relatively a high concentration (500-fold the concentration of methylated target DNA) was much lower than that by 100 fM target DNA. Thus, the integrated paper-based device exhibited high immunity from interference, exhibiting a desirable selectivity toward target DNA. The reproducibility of the ECL LOP device was investigated by using the same proposed device for the detection of 10 fM target DNA (Fig. 5F). The relative standard deviation (RSD) was 3.39% as expected, indicating that the integrated lab-on-paper systems was stable, reliable, and sensitive for detecting the target DNA.

To further verify the feasibility of the integrated ratiometric ECL device in complex sample matrix, the recovery experiments based on standard addition method were carried out using human blood serum samples. Specifically, different concentrations of methylated target DNA (20, 200, and 2000 fM) were added into the normal human serum diluted 10 times with PBS (0.1 M, pH 7.6). As presented in Table S2, clearly, the recovery was from 96.85% to 104.55%, with acceptable precision. Furthermore, the RSD values were all less than 4.86%, implying an acceptable accuracy. These above results manifest that the dual-potential ratiometric ECL integrated LOP device has great application potential for DNA methylation detection.

In summary, a ratiometric ECL integrated LOP device for high specific and sensitive detection of DNA methylation was constructed based on the excellent performance of copper paper electrode. Particularly, taking advantage of simple, low-cost solution-processing method based on polydopamine adhesion, a copper paper electrode with high conductivity was successfully prepared. Meanwhile, the one-to-multiple amplification effect between the target DNA and the signal probe was realized, which greatly contributed to enhance the GCAL ECL signal and quench the CQDs ECL signal. Thus, the proposed dual-potential ratiometric ECL analysis strategy enables accomplish recognition of methylated DNA, and provides a highly sensitive and accurate results, simultaneously. Considering the flexibility of lab-on-paper equipment, the outlined principles can be further extended to the determination of other biomarkers. Moreover, given that the excellent properties mentioned above, the integrated LOP device possessed great potential applications for on-site diagnosis of tumorigenesis in clinical analysis.

Xue Lv: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Lin Zhu: Investigation, Formal analysis. Yumeng Rong: Investigation, Visualization. Huihui Shi: Investigation, Data curation. Lina Zhang: Visualization, Conceptualization, Resources. Jinghua Yu: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Project administration. Yan Zhang: Investigation, Writing – review & editing, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21904047 and 21874055), Taishan Scholars Program (tsqn202103082), the Excellent Youth Innovation Team in Universities of Shandong (2021KJ021), Case-by-Case Project for Top Outstanding Talents of Jinan and the project of “20 items of University” of Jinan (2018GXRC001) are appreciated.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.bios.2022.114522.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Ali et al., 2019
ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Ali, M.M., Wolfe, M., Tram, K., Gu, J., Filipe, C.D.M., Li, Y., Brennan, J.D., 2019. Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 58, 9907-9911.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Cao et al., 2021
ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Cao, Y., Ma, C., Zhu, J.-J., 2021. J. Anal. Test. 5, 95-111.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Chen et al., 2022
ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Chen, D., Wu, Y., Tilley, R.D., Gooding, J.J., 2022. Biosens. Bioelectron. 206, 114126.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Chen et al., 2021
